text:
'Hotel Cosmopolitan Jewel Robbery. John Horner, 26 , plumber, was brought up upon the charge of having upon the 22 nd inst. abstracted from the jewel-case of the Countess of Morcar the valuable gem known as the blue carbuncle. James Ryder, upper-attendant at the hotel, gave his evidence to the effect that he had shown Horner up to the dressing-room of the Countess of Morcar upon the day of the robbery in order that he might solder the second bar of the grate, which was loose. He had remained with Horner some little time, but had finally been called away. On returning, he found that Horner had disappeared, that the bureau had been forced open, and that the small morocco casket in which, as it afterwards transpired, the Countess was accustomed to keep her jewel, was lying empty upon the dressing-table. Ryder instantly gave the alarm, and Horner was arrested the same evening; but the stone could not be found either upon his person or in his rooms. Catherine Cusack, maid to the Countess, deposed to having heard Ryder's cry of dismay on discovering the robbery, and to having rushed into the room, where she found matters as described by the last witness. Inspector Bradstreet, B division, gave evidence as to the arrest of Horner, who struggled frantically, and protested his innocence in the strongest terms. Evidence of a previous conviction for robbery having been given against the prisoner, the magistrate refused to deal summarily with the offence, but referred it to the Assizes. Horner, who had shown signs of intense emotion during the proceedings, fainted away at the conclusion and was carried out of court.'  
'Hum! So much for the police-court,' said Holmes thoughtfully, tossing aside the paper. 'The question for us now to solve is the sequence of events leading from a rifled jewel-case at one end to the crop of a goose in Tottenham Court Road at the other. You see, Watson, our little deductions have suddenly assumed a much more important and less innocent aspect. Here is the stone; the stone came from the goose, and the goose came from Mr. Henry Baker, the gentleman with the bad hat and all the other characteristics with which I have bored you. So now we must set ourselves very seriously to finding this gentleman and ascertaining what part he has played in this little mystery. To do this, we must try the simplest means first, and these lie undoubtedly in an advertisement in all the evening papers. If this fail, I shall have recourse to other methods.'  
'What will you say?'  
'Give me a pencil and that slip of paper. Now, then: 'Found at the corner of Goodge Street, a goose and a black felt hat. Mr. Henry Baker can have the same by applying at 6 . 30 this evening at 221b , Baker Street.' That is clear and concise.'  
'Very. But will he see it?'  
'Well, he is sure to keep an eye on the papers, since, to a poor man, the loss was a heavy one. He was clearly so scared by his mischance in breaking the window and by the approach of Peterson that he thought of nothing but flight, but since then he must have bitterly regretted the impulse which caused him to drop his bird. Then, again, the introduction of his name will cause him to see it, for everyone who knows him will direct his attention to it. Here you are, Peterson, run down to the advertising agency and have this put in the evening papers.'  
'In which, sir?'  
'Oh, in the Globe, Star, Pall Mall, St. James's, Evening News, Standard, Echo, and any others that occur to you.'  
'Very well, sir. And this stone?'  
'Ah, yes, I shall keep the stone. Thank you. And, I say, Peterson, just buy a goose on your way back and leave it here with me, for we must have one to give to this gentleman in place of the one which your family is now devouring.'  
When the commissionaire had gone, Holmes took up the stone and held it against the light. 'It's a bonny thing,' said he. 'Just see how it glints and sparkles. Of course it is a nucleus and focus of crime. Every good stone is. They are the devil's pet baits. In the larger and older jewels every facet may stand for a bloody deed. This stone is not yet twenty years old. It was found in the banks of the Amoy River in southern China and is remarkable in having every characteristic of the carbuncle, save that it is blue in shade instead of ruby red. In spite of its youth, it has already a sinister history. There have been two murders, a vitriol-throwing, a suicide, and several robberies brought about for the sake of this forty-grain weight of crystallised charcoal. Who would think that so pretty a toy would be a purveyor to the gallows and the prison? I'll lock it up in my strong box now and drop a line to the Countess to say that we have it.'  
'Do you think that this man Horner is innocent?'  
'I cannot tell.'  
'Well, then, do you imagine that this other one, Henry Baker, had anything to do with the matter?'  
'It is, I think, much more likely that Henry Baker is an absolutely innocent man, who had no idea that the bird which he was carrying was of considerably more value than if it were made of solid gold. That, however, I shall determine by a very simple test if we have an answer to our advertisement.'  
'And you can do nothing until then?'  
'Nothing.'  
'In that case I shall continue my professional round. But I shall come back in the evening at the hour you have mentioned, for I should like to see the solution of so tangled a business.'  
'Very glad to see you. I dine at seven. There is a woodcock, I believe. By the way, in view of recent occurrences, perhaps I ought to ask Mrs. Hudson to examine its crop.'  
I had been delayed at a case, and it was a little after half-past six when I found myself in Baker Street once more. As I approached the house I saw a tall man in a Scotch bonnet with a coat which was buttoned up to his chin waiting outside in the bright semicircle which was thrown from the fanlight. Just as I arrived the door was opened, and we were shown up together to Holmes' room.  
'Mr. Henry Baker, I believe,' said he, rising from his armchair and greeting his visitor with the easy air of geniality which he could so readily assume. 'Pray take this chair by the fire, Mr. Baker. It is a cold night, and I observe that your circulation is more adapted for summer than for winter. Ah, Watson, you have just come at the right time. Is that your hat, Mr. Baker?'  
'Yes, sir, that is undoubtedly my hat.'  
He was a large man with rounded shoulders, a massive head, and a broad, intelligent face, sloping down to a pointed beard of grizzled brown. A touch of red in nose and cheeks, with a slight tremor of his extended hand, recalled Holmes' surmise as to his habits. His rusty black frock-coat was buttoned right up in front, with the collar turned up, and his lank wrists protruded from his sleeves without a sign of cuff or shirt. He spoke in a slow staccato fashion, choosing his words with care, and gave the impression generally of a man of learning and letters who had had ill-usage at the hands of fortune.  
'We have retained these things for some days,' said Holmes, 'because we expected to see an advertisement from you giving your address. I am at a loss to know now why you did not advertise.'
name: Chunk 3/7

context: Evidence of a previous conviction for robbery having been given against the prisoner, the magistrate refused to deal summarily with the offence, but referred it to the Assizes.

entities.name: Innocence vs. Guilt
entities.description: A central thematic tension in the narrative, explored through the wrongful accusation of John Horner and Holmes's suspicion that Henry Baker is an innocent pawn unaware of the jewel hidden in his goose.
context: who struggled frantically, and protested his innocence in the strongest terms.
It is, I think, much more likely that Henry Baker is an absolutely innocent man, who had no idea that the bird which he was carrying was of considerably more value than if it were made of solid gold.

entities.name: Sinister History of the Gem
entities.description: The dark legacy of the blue carbuncle, which Holmes notes has already caused two murders, a vitriol-throwing, a suicide, and several robberies, framing the gem as a corrupting force.
context: This stone is not yet twenty years old. It was found in the banks of the Amoy River in southern China and is remarkable in having every characteristic of the carbuncle, save that it is blue in shade instead of ruby red. In spite of its youth, it has already a sinister history.
There have been two murders, a vitriol-throwing, a suicide, and several robberies brought about for the sake of this forty-grain weight of crystallised charcoal.

entities.name: Deduction and Investigation
entities.description: The methodological approach Holmes employs, relying on logical inference from physical evidence (the goose, the hat) and strategic public notices to unravel the sequence of events surrounding the theft.
context: You see, Watson, our little deductions have suddenly assumed a much more important and less innocent aspect.
To do this, we must try the simplest means first, and these lie undoubtedly in an advertisement in all the evening papers.

properties.discovery: found bureau forced open, casket empty

properties.background: man of learning and letters ill-used by fortune

properties.location: found at corner of Goodge Street

properties.text: Found at the corner of Goodge Street, a goose and a black felt hat. Mr. Henry Baker can have the same by applying at 6.30 this evening at 221b, Baker Street.

properties.outcome: jewel-case rifled, casket empty, Horner arrested
referred to Assizes due to prior conviction, Horner fainted
properties.significance: establishes legal stakes and Horner's emotional state

properties.purpose: verify Baker's identity and investigate the goose
properties.outcome: Baker confirms hat ownership, Holmes begins inquiry

properties.theme: judging character based on evidence vs. appearance
embodiment: Horner's prior conviction vs. protest of innocence
properties.resolution: Holmes suspects Baker is innocent despite circumstances

properties.description: gem has caused two murders, vitriol-throwing, suicide, robberies
properties.quote: purveyor to the gallows and the prison

properties.method: observing physical details to infer habits
properties.example: Holmes deduces Baker's circulation, speech, and background from appearance
properties.process: advertising as a test, following the sequence from jewel-case to goose

relationships.description: Horner is charged with stealing the Countess's jewel, implying a fraudulent or criminal act against her property.
relationships.context: abstracted from the jewel-case of the Countess of Morcar the valuable gem known as the blue carbuncle.

relationships.description: Ryder, as an upper-attendant, escorted Horner to the Countess's dressing-room to perform repairs.
relationships.context: shown Horner up to the dressing-room of the Countess of Morcar upon the day of the robbery in order that he might solder the second bar of the grate

relationships.description: Cusack serves as the personal maid to the Countess.
relationships.context: Catherine Cusack, maid to the Countess, deposed to having heard Ryder's cry of dismay

relationships.description: Bradstreet is an inspector assigned to the B Division of the police force.
relationships.context: Inspector Bradstreet, B division, gave evidence as to the arrest of Horner

relationships.description: Holmes takes on the case to solve the sequence of events surrounding the jewel theft.
relationships.context: The question for us now to solve is the sequence of events leading from a rifled jewel-case at one end to the crop of a goose in Tottenham Court Road at the other.

relationships.description: Holmes seeks to determine Henry Baker's involvement in the mystery surrounding the stolen gem.
relationships.context: So now we must set ourselves very seriously to finding this gentleman and ascertaining what part he has played in this little mystery.

relationships.description: Watson collaborates with Holmes on the case, sharing deductions and assisting in the investigation.
relationships.context: You see, Watson, our little deductions have suddenly assumed a much more important and less innocent aspect.

relationships.description: Peterson is tasked by Holmes to place an advertisement in the evening papers.
relationships.context: Here you are, Peterson, run down to the advertising agency and have this put in the evening papers.

relationships.description: Holmes takes possession of the recovered gem to secure it and study it.
relationships.context: I shall keep the stone. Thank you.

relationships.description: Holmes uses his personal strong box to store the valuable Blue Carbuncle.
relationships.context: I'll lock it up in my strong box now and drop a line to the Countess to say that we have it.

relationships.description: Holmes intends to notify the Countess that the stolen gem has been recovered.
relationships.context: drop a line to the Countess to say that we have it.

relationships.description: Watson questions Holmes regarding the moral standing and guilt of John Horner.
relationships.context: Do you think that this man Horner is innocent? I cannot tell.

relationships.description: Holmes assesses Henry Baker's character and involvement, leaning toward his innocence.
relationships.context: It is, I think, much more likely that Henry Baker is an absolutely innocent man, who had no idea that the bird which he was carrying was of considerably more value than if it were made of solid gold.

relationships.description: Watson returns to Holmes's residence in the evening to observe the case resolution.
relationships.context: I had been delayed at a case, and it was a little after half-past six when I found myself in Baker Street once more.

relationships.description: Henry Baker claims ownership of the black felt hat found with the goose.
relationships.context: Yes, sir, that is undoubtedly my hat.

relationships.description: Holmes lives and operates his detective practice from this address.
relationships.context: applying at 6.30 this evening at 221b, Baker Street.

relationships.description: Ryder works and lives at the Hotel Cosmopolitan as an upper-attendant.
relationships.context: James Ryder, upper-attendant at the hotel, gave his evidence

relationships.description: Horner is the defendant in the police court trial regarding the jewel theft.
relationships.context: Horner, who had shown signs of intense emotion during the proceedings, fainted away at the conclusion and was carried out of court.

relationships.description: The Assizes are tasked with determining the legal guilt or innocence in the case.
relationships.context: the magistrate refused to deal summarily with the offence, but referred it to the Assizes.

relationships.description: Holmes reviews the police court proceedings to understand the official narrative and evidence.
relationships.context: Hum! So much for the police-court,' said Holmes thoughtfully, tossing aside the paper.

relationships.description: Holmes examines the gem closely, noting its physical properties and criminal history.
relationships.context: When the commissionaire had gone, Holmes took up the stone and held it against the light. 'It's a bonny thing,' said he.

relationships.description: Watson shares his professional duties and case updates with Holmes, relying on his partnership.
relationships.context: In that case I shall continue my professional round. But I shall come back in the evening at the hour you have mentioned, for I should like to see the solution of so tangled a business.

relationships.description: Henry Baker travels to Baker Street in response to the advertisement to claim his lost items.
relationships.context: Mr. Henry Baker can have the same by applying at 6.30 this evening at 221b, Baker Street.

relationships.description: Holmes questions Henry Baker directly to verify his story and assess his innocence.
relationships.context: I am at a loss to know now why you did not advertise.

relationships.description: Holmes acquires a goose to replace the one Peterson's family consumed, intending to use it for the investigation.
relationships.context: And, I say, Peterson, just buy a goose on your way back and leave it here with me, for we must have one to give to this gentleman in place of the one which your family is now devouring.

relationships.description: The Morocco Casket is used to store the Blue Carbuncle, linking the object to the crime scene.
relationships.context: the small morocco casket in which, as it afterwards transpired, the Countess was accustomed to keep her jewel, was lying empty upon the dressing-table.

relationships.description: The Blue Carbuncle originates from the banks of the Amoy River in southern China.
relationships.context: This stone is not yet twenty years old. It was found in the banks of the Amoy River in southern China

relationships.description: Holmes utilizes the Evening Papers as a medium to place an advertisement and attract Henry Baker.
relationships.context: these lie undoubtedly in an advertisement in all the evening papers.

relationships.description: Holmes initiates the advertisement placement to solve the mystery and recover the goose.
relationships.context: To do this, we must try the simplest means first, and these lie undoubtedly in an advertisement in all the evening papers.

relationships.description: Watson accompanies Holmes to the meeting with Henry Baker to observe the case resolution.
relationships.context: Just as I arrived the door was opened, and we were shown up together to Holmes' room.

relationships.description: Holmes hosts and conducts the meeting with Henry Baker to verify his identity and story.
relationships.context: greeting his visitor with the easy air of geniality which he could so readily assume.
